questions.question: Which early computer was described as a control room with eight banks of tubes and could add two sixteen-bit words in two microseconds? (FDE Slide 1)
The Memory Test Computer used a magnetic-core memory bank that stored how many sixteen-bit words? (FDE Slide 2)
What key principle is highlighted by the term 'stored program' in computing? (FDE Slide 3)
options: Instructions are stored separately from data
Software instructions are treated as data stored in memory
Programs must be loaded from external storage at every execution

questions.question: Which of the following sequences correctly lists the three phases of machine execution? (FDE Slide 4)
During the fetch cycle, what component determines the address of the next instruction? (FDE Slide 5)
What is the correct order of actions when executing a single instruction? (FDE Slide 6)
options: Fetch → decode → get data (if needed) → execute

questions.question: In the musical performance analogy, which step corresponds to the 'decode' phase of machine execution? (FDE Slide 7)
How many data registers does the Little Man Computer have? (FDE Slide 8)
Which LMC instruction halts program execution? (FDE Slide 9)
In Challenge 1 (Sum of two numbers), what is the purpose of the instruction STA 99 after the first INP? (FDE Slide 10)
options: It stores the input value at memory location 99 for later addition
It stores the sum of both numbers in location 99
It adds the value in location 99 to the accumulator

questions.question: In Challenge 2 (Multiplication), why does the code use BRZ 14? (FDE Slide 11)
options: To skip the loop if the product is zero
To branch to the end if the counter (at RAM 98) is zero
To load the accumulator with zero

questions.question: When SUB 96 is executed in the LMC multiplication program, what is being subtracted? (FDE Slide 12)
options: The decrement value (1) stored in location 96

questions.question: Which statement best summarizes what the Fetch–Decode–Execute lecture aims to teach? (FDE Slide 13)
options: How complex instruction sets are implemented in hardware
The concept of machine execution and how to trace the FDE cycle using the Little Man Computer
The importance of pipelining in modern CPUs
The basics of binary and hexadecimal conversions

questions.question: Which processor marked the origin of the x86 family in 1978? (ISA Slide 1)
Which learning outcome is not explicitly mentioned for Module 4? (ISA Slide 2)
options: Trace machine execution through modern computer architecture from assembly instructions
Trace execution of software from C statements to machine instructions
Understand the history of computing from ENIAC to Pentium

questions.question: Which statement about the 1970s microprocessor revolution is correct? (ISA Slide 3)
options: The Intel 8008 was the first commercial microprocessor
The Intel 4004 and 8008 were introduced in 1971 and 1972, respectively
VLSI technology in the 1970s decreased circuit density
Microprocessors were first adopted in the 1990s

questions.question: Which feature is not listed as an improvement of the 1993 Pentium processor? (ISA Slide 4)
options: Execution of at least two instructions per clock cycle

questions.question: What is a primary objective of CISC processors? (ISA Slide 5)
options: Minimize program size by decreasing the number of instructions
Reduce the number of transistors needed

questions.question: Which characteristic is associated with CISC architectures? (ISA Slide 6)
options: Multi-clock, memory-to-memory instructions with small code sizes

questions.question: Which is an advantage of CISC architecture? (ISA Slide 7)
options: Lower cost due to smaller chips
More complex code easier to write and smaller in size

questions.question: One disadvantage of CISC processors is that they: (ISA Slide 8)
options: Require more transistors and are typically larger and more expensive
Have slower memory because of Harvard architecture

questions.question: Which company's research in the 1970s led to the reduced instruction set computer (RISC) concept? (ISA Slide 9)
RISC architectures typically employ which of the following? (ISA Slide 10)
options: Variable-length instructions with many addressing modes
Small register files and direct memory access in arithmetic
Simple fixed-length instructions, more registers and pipelining
Emulation of CISC instructions for compatibility

questions.question: Which is a benefit of RISC processors? (ISA Slide 11)
options: They are more expensive to manufacture
They simplify hardware design and can use smaller chips
They reduce the number of pipeline stages needed
They make code small and less reliant on memory caches

questions.question: RISC architectures suffer from which of the following limitations? (ISA Slide 12)
options: Slow execution due to complex instructions
Dependence on very fast memory systems and large caches

questions.question: When adding C = A + B, why does the RISC (MIPS) example require more instructions than the x86 (CISC) example? (ISA Slide 13)
options: Because MIPS must use immediate values for all additions
Because MIPS instructions must load and store operands separately and operate only on registers
Because x86 only supports two registers for arithmetic
Because x86 cannot access memory directly

questions.question: Which x86 register is typically used for loop counting and iteration? (ISA Slide 14)
Which register points to the top of the stack in x86? (ISA Slide 15)
Which of these flags is not part of the EFLAGS register in x86? (ISA Slide 16)
The Instruction Sets & Registers lecture concludes by emphasizing an understanding of: (ISA Slide 17)
options: CISC vs. RISC instruction sets and x86 registers
Assembly programming for the Little Man Computer

questions.question: Why is the mov instruction considered fundamental in x86 assembly? (Assembly Slide 1)
options: It performs arithmetic operations directly on memory
It initializes variables and loads constants and data into registers
It compares two values and sets the zero flag
It jumps to different parts of the program based on conditions

questions.question: What determines the opcode for a mov instruction? (Assembly Slide 2)
options: The combination of source and destination operands

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid mov instruction format? (Assembly Slide 3)
What does the mov instruction do to its first operand? (Assembly Slide 4)
options: It clears the first operand after copying
It does not change the first operand; it copies its value to the destination
It stores the first operand as an immediate value

questions.question: In the example that multiplies 4 × 2 and prints the result, what instruction is used for multiplication? (Assembly Slide 5)
Which instruction triggers a Linux system call in the multiplication example? (Assembly Slide 6)
Which instruction would you use to move a 16-bit (word) data item? (Assembly Slide 7)
Which of the following statements correctly contrasts registers and memory addresses? (Assembly Slide 8)
options: Registers are slower and store data for longer periods; memory addresses are faster and temporary
Registers are small, fast, temporary storage units within the CPU, while memory addresses refer to main memory locations for longer-term storage
Registers can only be accessed by system calls; memory addresses can be accessed directly by instructions
Memory addresses are part of the CPU; registers are in RAM

questions.question: A double-word operand (32 bits) can represent integer values ranging from 0 to what maximum? (Assembly Slide 9)
What does the Assembly Instructions lecture mainly focus on? (Assembly Slide 10)
options: Implementing entire programs in high-level languages
Learning about the LMC programming model
Understanding x86 assembly instructions, operand formats and basic arithmetic and system calls
Exploring the history of the Pentium processor

questions.question: What learning outcome is achieved through the Branching lecture? (Branching Slide 1)
options: Trace machine execution from assembly instructions through a modern computer architecture
Learn about pipelining and branch prediction only

questions.question: Which lecture session is not part of Module 4? (Branching Slide 2)
Why is the fetch–decode–execute cycle described as 'highly predictable'? (Branching Slide 3)
options: Because it uses random memory access
Because it always follows the same sequence unless a branch occurs
Because modern CPUs have unpredictable timing

questions.question: In the provided C function, under what condition is B incremented by 2? (Branching Slide 4)
options: When A is greater than B

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the role of the EFLAGS register? (Branching Slide 5)
options: It stores the address of the next instruction to be executed
It stores status flags that reflect the outcome of arithmetic and logical operations
It holds immediate values for arithmetic operations

questions.question: In the assembly translation of the if (A != 0) statement, what instruction checks whether A equals zero? (Branching Slide 6)
Branching in assembly language allows a program to: (Branching Slide 7)
options: Execute different sequences of instructions based on conditions
Increase the size of the program counter

questions.question: Which of the following jump instructions transfers control unconditionally? (Branching Slide 8)
Which flag is set by cmpl when the subtraction result is negative? (Branching Slide 9)
What does the jne instruction do? (Branching Slide 10)
options: Jumps if the zero flag is set
Jumps if the zero flag is not set
Jumps if the sign flag is set
Jumps if the carry flag is set

questions.question: Why are labels used in assembly code? (Branching Slide 11)
options: To indicate locations without specifying actual memory addresses at compile time
To mark the beginning of functions in high-level languages
To store the values of local variables

questions.question: In the translated if–else assembly code, what instruction prevents the execution of the 'else' part if A is not zero? (Branching Slide 12)
In the assembly translation of while (A <= 0), what instruction is used to exit the loop when A becomes positive? (Branching Slide 13)
Which of the following control structures uses two unconditional jumps (one for call and one for return)? (Branching Slide 14)
What distinguishes conditional branching from unconditional branching? (Branching Slide 15)
options: Conditional branching jumps unconditionally; unconditional branching uses flags
Conditional branching evaluates a condition before jumping; unconditional branching always jumps regardless of conditions
Both require the zero flag to be set

questions.question: Branching can impact performance because modern processors use what technique to guess the outcome of branches and reduce pipeline stalls? (Branching Slide 16)
Which CPU component decodes the opcode and determines which operation to perform?
What role does the arithmetic logic unit (ALU) play in the fetch–decode–execute cycle?
options: It performs the actual arithmetic or logic operations during execution

questions.question: In the fetch phase, the CPU retrieves an instruction from memory based on which register?
What happens if a branch instruction alters the program counter during the fetch–decode–execute cycle?
options: The normal PC+1 sequence is interrupted, and the next instruction comes from the branch target

questions.question: An instruction set defines:
options: The hardware components of the CPU
The basic commands a CPU can execute, serving as a bridge between software and hardware
The programming languages a CPU can understand

questions.question: Which of the following is part of a CPU's instruction set?
options: A set of commands covering arithmetic, data transfer, and control operations

questions.question: Which architecture uses complex instructions that often require multiple cycles to execute and can operate directly on memory?
Which architecture typically uses fixed-length instructions, emphasizes registers over memory and is well-suited for pipelining?
Which register holds the address of the next instruction to execute in x86?
What is the primary purpose of the EFLAGS register?
options: It stores various status flags (zero, sign, carry, overflow) that indicate the results of operations

questions.question: Which type of operand can appear in an x86 instruction?
What does the add instruction do in x86 assembly? (Assume add <source>,<dest>)
options: Adds source to destination and stores the result in the destination

questions.question: In x86 assembly, which of the following is a valid immediate operand format?
Which instruction sets the zero flag if the operands are equal and is typically followed by a conditional jump?
Branching instructions change the program counter to:
options: A different instruction address based on a condition or unconditionally

questions.question: Which of the following is not a scenario where branching is used?
options: Simple sequential arithmetic operations without any decision

questions.question: Which branch instruction is used to implement a loop condition in assembly?
options: jg or a similar conditional jump to exit the loop

questions.question: In a high-level if–else structure compiled to assembly, how many jumps are typically used?
options: One conditional jump and one unconditional jump

questions.question: Which jump instruction transfers control unconditionally?
Which conditional jump tests the zero flag and transfers control if it is set?
Which of the following best encapsulates the major difference between RISC and CISC architectures?
options: RISC executes instructions more slowly because it has fewer registers
CISC includes many simple instructions executed in a single cycle
RISC uses simple instructions that rely on registers and can execute in one cycle; CISC uses complex, multi-cycle instructions that can operate directly on memory
They are identical except for historical branding

questions.question: Why do RISC processors typically require larger and faster caches than CISC processors?
options: Because RISC instructions are decoded more slowly
Because RISC compilers generate more complex microcode
Because the rapid execution rate of simple RISC instructions demands fast memory access to keep the pipeline fed
Because RISC processors store data in caches instead of registers

questions.question: Which x86 register is not a general-purpose register but is crucial for managing function calls and returning to the correct location?
In terms of control flow, what do loops, if–else structures and function calls have in common?
options: They all require branching instructions to alter the normal sequential flow of execution
They all use the int 0x80 instruction
They are executed outside the CPU

questions.question: Which pipeline hazard is most directly affected by branch instructions?
What is the purpose of using labels in assembly programs?
options: They provide symbolic names for memory addresses, making it easier to reference jump targets

questions.question: Which of the following correctly pairs an x86 instruction with its typical use?
options: add – transfer control based on flags
jne – conditional jump that occurs if the zero flag is clear

questions.question: Which of the following is not directly visible to an assembly programmer?
If a program must multiply two numbers, which combination of instructions is generally used in x86?
Which step is not part of the fetch–decode–execute cycle?
options: Compile the instruction into high-level code

questions.question: What is a key reason that high-level languages use conditionals like if instead of direct cmp/jne instructions?
options: Conditionals abstract away low-level control flow and make code easier to write and maintain
cmp/jne are unavailable on modern CPUs
High-level languages are slower than assembly

questions.question: In the context of x86, which statement about immediate values is true?
options: Immediate values must be stored in memory before use
Immediate values are denoted by a dollar sign ($) and can be used as operands directly
Immediate values can only be used for branching instructions
Immediate values always represent memory addresses

questions.question: Which pipeline technique is used by both RISC and CISC processors to improve throughput by overlapping instruction stages?
Which of the following is not part of the EFLAGS register used for conditional jumps?
Which is a correct description of mov %ebx, %eax?
options: It moves the value in %eax into %ebx
It moves the value in %ebx into %eax
It swaps the values of %eax and %ebx

questions.question: Which mov instruction stores an 8-bit value of 5 into a variable var?
What does the sub instruction do? (Assume sub <source>,<dest>)
options: Adds source and destination and stores in source
Subtracts the destination from the source and stores in the destination
Subtracts source from destination and stores in the destination

questions.question: Which instruction multiplies the value in %ebx by the value in %eax?
When cmp $0, %eax is executed, what happens if %eax equals 0?
What is the purpose of the parity flag (PF)?
options: Indicates whether the number of set bits in the result is even
Indicates if there was a borrow in an unsigned subtraction
Indicates if the result is negative
Indicates if there is an overflow

questions.question: Which instruction causes the program to jump if the sign flag is clear?
Which conditional jump would be used to branch when a value is greater than zero?
Which register holds the Linux system call number for SYS_WRITE in the multiplication program example?
Which register typically contains the file descriptor when invoking a Linux write system call?
After executing: mov $7, %eax; add $5, %eax - What is the value in %eax?
After executing: mov $10, %eax; mov $3, %ebx; sub %ebx, %eax - What value does %eax hold?
After executing: mov $5, %eax; imul $4, %eax; add $1, %eax - What is %eax?
After executing: mov $2, %eax; mov $5, %ebx; imul %ebx, %eax; sub $4, %eax - What is %eax?
After executing: mov $8, %eax; mov $3, %ecx; add %ecx, %eax; imul $2, %eax - What is the final value of %eax?
Assume num1=4 and num2=6. After executing: mov num1, %eax; add num2, %eax - What is %eax?
After executing: mov $10, %eax; mov $4, %ebx; mov %eax, %ecx; sub %ebx, %ecx; add %ebx, %eax - What is %eax?
After executing: mov $3, %eax; mov $2, %ebx; imul %ebx, %eax; mov $5, %ecx; add %ecx, %eax - What is %eax?
After executing: mov $12, %eax; mov $3, %ebx; add %ebx, %eax; sub $5, %eax - What is %eax?
After executing: mov $2, %eax; mov $3, %ebx; add %ebx, %eax; imul $3, %eax; sub $2, %eax - What is the final value of %eax?
Which instruction increments a 32-bit register by one without requiring an immediate operand?
What does the instruction shll $1, %ebx accomplish?
options: Multiplies %ebx by 2 by shifting left one bit

questions.question: Which of the following x86 instructions performs a bitwise AND between two registers?
To load integers from memory, increment one using incl, double the other using shll $1, and add them - which sequence is correct?
options: mov 1000, %eax; mov 2000, %ebx; incl %eax; shll $1, %ebx; mov %eax, %ecx; addl %ebx, %ecx;
mov 1000, %eax; mov 2000, %ebx; incl %ebx; shll $1, %eax; mov %eax, %ecx; addl %ebx, %ecx;
mov 1000, %eax; mov 2000, %ebx; incw %eax; shll $2, %ebx; mov %eax, %ecx; addl %ebx, %ecx;

questions.question: Which of the following qualities is characteristic of a RISC instruction set?
options: The CPU control is mostly hard-wired without control memory
The instruction set contains a large number of variable-length instructions
Instructions are complex and may perform multiple operations
Instructions routinely require multiple clock cycles to execute

questions.question: Define: mov instruction
options: Moves data from source to destination (copies, doesn't remove from source)
Compares two values and sets flags
Jumps to a new instruction address

questions.question: Define: add instruction
options: Adds source to destination and stores result in destination

questions.question: Define: sub instruction
options: Subtracts source from destination and stores result in destination

questions.question: Define: imul instruction
Define: cmp instruction
options: Compares two operands by subtracting them and setting flags (doesn't store result)

questions.question: Define: jmp instruction
options: Jumps only if zero flag is set
Jumps if values are not equal
Conditional jump based on sign flag

questions.question: Define: je (or jz) instruction
options: Jump if equal (zero flag is set)

questions.question: Define: jne (or jnz) instruction
options: Jump if not equal (zero flag is clear)

questions.question: Define: jg instruction
options: Jump if general purpose flag is set

questions.question: Define: jl instruction
Define: js instruction
options: Jump if sign flag is set (result was negative)

questions.question: Define: jns instruction
options: Jump if sign flag is clear (result was non-negative)

questions.question: Define: incl instruction
Define: decl instruction
Define: ZF (Zero Flag)
options: Set when result of operation is zero
Set when there's a zero division error
Set when zero register is used

questions.question: Define: SF (Sign Flag)
options: Set when a signed operation occurs
Set when result is negative (most significant bit is 1)

questions.question: Define: CF (Carry Flag)
options: Set when there is a carry out or borrow in unsigned arithmetic

questions.question: Define: OF (Overflow Flag)
options: Set when signed arithmetic result is too large/small to fit in operand

questions.question: Define: %eax register
options: General purpose accumulator register, often used for return values and arithmetic

questions.question: Define: %ebx register
options: General purpose base register, often used for memory addressing

questions.question: Define: %ecx register
options: General purpose counter register, commonly used for loop counting

questions.question: Define: %edx register
options: General purpose data register, often used in multiplication/division

questions.question: Define: %esp register
options: Stack pointer - points to top of the stack

questions.question: Define: %ebp register
options: Base pointer - points to base of current stack frame

questions.question: Define: immediate value (e.g., $5)
options: A constant literal value prefixed with $ that is part of the instruction itself
A value stored immediately in memory
A value that must be calculated immediately
A value stored in the accumulator

questions.question: Define: label in assembly
options: A symbolic name for a memory address/location in code

questions.question: Define: EFLAGS register
options: A register that stores status flags (ZF, SF, CF, OF, etc.) reflecting results of operations
A register for extended flags only

questions.question: Translate to assembly: int a = 5;
Translate to assembly: a = a + 3; (assume a is in %eax)
Translate to assembly: b = b - 2; (assume b is in %ebx)
Translate to assembly: c = a + b; (a in %eax, b in %ebx, c in %ecx)
Translate to assembly: a = a * 2; (assume a is in %eax)
Translate to assembly: a++; (assume a is in %eax)
Translate to assembly: b--; (assume b is in %ebx)
Translate condition check: if (a == 0) (assume a is in %eax)
Translate condition check: if (a != 0) (assume a is in %eax)
Translate: if (a > 0) (assume a is in %eax)
Translate: if (a < 0) (assume a is in %eax)
Translate: if (a >= 0) (assume a is in %eax)
Simple if statement: if (a != 0) { b = 5; } Full translation pattern?
options: cmp $0, %eax; jne do_it; jmp skip; do_it: mov $5, %ebx; skip:

questions.question: If-else: if (a == 0) { b = 1; } else { b = 2; } What's the pattern?
options: cmp $0, %eax; je else_part; mov $1, %ebx; jmp end; else_part: mov $2, %ebx; end:
cmp $0, %eax; je then_part; mov $2, %ebx; jmp end; then_part: mov $1, %ebx; end:
cmp $0, %eax; jne then_part; mov $2, %ebx; then_part: mov $1, %ebx;
mov $0, %eax; je else_part; mov $1, %ebx; else_part: mov $2, %ebx;

questions.question: While loop start: while (a > 0) What's the pattern?
options: loop_start: cmp $0, %eax; jle loop_end; [body]; jmp loop_start; loop_end:

questions.question: While loop: while (a < 10) { a++; } Full translation?
options: loop: cmp $10, %eax; jge end; incl %eax; jmp loop; end:
loop: cmp $10, %eax; jl loop; incl %eax; end:

questions.question: Translate: a = 4; b = 5; while (a < b) { b++; }
options: mov $4, %eax; mov $5, %ebx; loop: cmp %ebx, %eax; jge end; incl %ebx; jmp loop; end:
mov $4, %eax; mov $5, %ebx; loop: cmp %eax, %ebx; jl end; incl %ebx; jmp loop; end:
mov $4, %eax; mov $5, %ebx; loop: incl %ebx; cmp %ebx, %eax; jl loop;
mov $4, %eax; mov $5, %ebx; incl %ebx; loop: cmp %ebx, %eax; jge end; jmp loop; end:

questions.question: Translate: int x = 10; while (x > 0) { x--; }
options: mov $10, %eax; loop: cmp $0, %eax; jle end; decl %eax; jmp loop; end:
mov $10, %eax; loop: decl %eax; cmp $0, %eax; jg loop;
mov $10, %eax; loop: cmp $0, %eax; jg loop; decl %eax; end:
mov $10, %eax; decl %eax; loop: cmp $0, %eax; jg loop;

questions.question: Translate: if (a == b) { c = 1; } (a in %eax, b in %ebx, c in %ecx)
Translate: if (a != b) { c = 0; } else { c = 1; }
options: cmp %ebx, %eax; je else_part; mov $0, %ecx; jmp end; else_part: mov $1, %ecx; end:
cmp %ebx, %eax; jne then_part; mov $1, %ecx; jmp end; then_part: mov $0, %ecx; end:
cmp %eax, %ebx; jne skip; mov $0, %ecx; skip: mov $1, %ecx;
mov %ebx, %eax; jne else_part; mov $0, %ecx; else_part: mov $1, %ecx;

questions.question: Translate: a = 0; while (a < 5) { a = a + 2; }
options: mov $0, %eax; loop: cmp $5, %eax; jge end; add $2, %eax; jmp loop; end:
mov $0, %eax; loop: add $2, %eax; cmp $5, %eax; jl loop;
mov $0, %eax; add $2, %eax; loop: cmp $5, %eax; jl loop;
mov $0, %eax; loop: cmp $5, %eax; jl end; add $2, %eax; jmp loop; end:

questions.question: Translate: if (a > b) { c = a; } else { c = b; } (max operation)
options: cmp %ebx, %eax; jle else_part; mov %eax, %ecx; jmp end; else_part: mov %ebx, %ecx; end:
cmp %ebx, %eax; jg then_part; mov %ebx, %ecx; jmp end; then_part: mov %eax, %ecx; end:
cmp %eax, %ebx; jg else_part; mov %eax, %ecx; else_part: mov %ebx, %ecx;
Both A and B are correct

questions.question: Translate: a = 3; b = a * 4;
Translate: while (a <= 10) { a++; } (Note <= not <)
options: loop: cmp $10, %eax; jg end; incl %eax; jmp loop; end:
loop: cmp $10, %eax; jle loop; incl %eax; end:
loop: cmp $10, %eax; jl end; incl %eax; jmp loop; end:

questions.question: Translate: if (a >= 5) { b = 10; }
options: cmp $5, %eax; jge then; jmp skip; then: mov $10, %ebx; skip:

questions.question: What's wrong with: if (a == 0) b++; translated as: mov $0, %eax; je skip; incl %ebx; skip:
options: Should use jne instead of je
Should use jmp instead of je

questions.question: Translate: a = b + c - d; (b in %ebx, c in %ecx, d in %edx, result in %eax)
Translate: if (a < 0) { a = 0; } (clamp to zero)
options: cmp $0, %eax; js set_zero; jmp skip; set_zero: mov $0, %eax; skip:
Both A and C are correct

questions.question: Translate: a = 10; b = 3; c = a - b;
Translate: if (a > 10) { b = 0; }
options: cmp $10, %eax; jg then; jmp skip; then: mov $0, %ebx; skip:
Both A and B are correct

questions.question: Translate: while (a != 5) { a++; }
options: loop: cmp $5, %eax; je end; incl %eax; jmp loop; end:

questions.question: Translate: a = b * 3;
options: Both A and C are correct

questions.question: Translate: if (a == b) { c = 10; } else { c = 20; }
options: cmp %ebx, %eax; je then; mov $20, %ecx; jmp end; then: mov $10, %ecx; end:
cmp %ebx, %eax; jne else; mov $10, %ecx; jmp end; else: mov $20, %ecx; end:
cmp %eax, %ebx; je then; mov $20, %ecx; then: mov $10, %ecx;
Both A and B are correct

questions.question: Translate: a = 1; while (a <= 5) { a = a + 1; }
options: mov $1, %eax; loop: cmp $5, %eax; jg end; add $1, %eax; jmp loop; end:
mov $1, %eax; loop: cmp $5, %eax; jle body; jmp end; body: add $1, %eax; jmp loop; end:
mov $1, %eax; loop: add $1, %eax; cmp $5, %eax; jle loop;
Both A and B are correct

questions.question: Translate: a = a - 1;
options: All of the above are correct

questions.question: Translate: if (a <= 0) { b = a; }
options: cmp $0, %eax; jle then; jmp skip; then: mov %eax, %ebx; skip:
cmp $0, %eax; js then; je then; jmp skip; then: mov %eax, %ebx; skip:
Both A and B are correct

questions.question: Translate: a = 0; b = 0; while (a < 3) { b = b + a; a++; }
options: mov $0, %eax; mov $0, %ebx; loop: cmp $3, %eax; jge end; add %eax, %ebx; incl %eax; jmp loop; end:
mov $0, %eax; mov $0, %ebx; loop: add %eax, %ebx; incl %eax; cmp $3, %eax; jl loop;
mov $0, %eax; mov $0, %ebx; loop: cmp $3, %eax; jl body; jmp end; body: add %eax, %ebx; incl %eax; jmp loop; end:
